title: NOC 70020 – Managers in transportation | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Managers in Transportation (NOC 70020) are the backbone of Canada’s supply chain, overseeing the complex movement of people and goods across air, rail, sea, and road. As the industry faces a "Neutral but Fragile" economic outlook in 2026, these leaders are prioritizing Smart Infrastructure and AI-driven logistics to combat rising operational costs and labor shortages. Whether managing a municipal transit system or a global freight forwarding company, transportation managers in 2026 are increasingly focused on sustainability, "Net-Zero" fleet transitions, and high-tech route optimization.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category0 (Management occupations)Major Group70 – Middle management occupations in trades and transportationSub-Major Group700 – Middle management occupations in trades and transportation

common_job_titles: Operations side: Airline Manager, Bus Company Manager, Railway Manager, Flight Operations Manager, Marine Superintendent.
Freight side: Director of Logistics, Distribution Manager, Freight Forwarding Manager, Export Traffic Manager, Corporate Transportation Manager.
duties: Operations Management: Planning and evaluating the operations of transportation companies (rail, air, bus, etc.) under the direction of senior management.
Policy & Safety: Setting standards for handling dangerous goods and ensuring strict compliance with federal and provincial transport regulations.
Budgetary Oversight: Controlling departmental budgets, including major acquisitions of vehicles, vessels, or aircraft.
Freight Coordination: Arranging shipping documentation and overseeing the schedule, dispatch, and real-time tracking of global goods.
2026 Update: Managing the integration of electric and autonomous vehicle fleets into existing logistics networks.
employment_requirements: Education: A bachelor's degree in Business Administration or Engineering is usually required for operations roles. Freight traffic roles often require a college diploma or university degree in Logistics/Transportation Administration.
Experience: Several years of supervisory experience in transportation are mandatory.
Alternative Route: Extensive experience as an operator (e.g., pilot, train engineer, or truck driver) combined with supervisory roles can sometimes substitute for formal education.
Certification: Mode-specific certification (e.g., Commercial Pilot License or Vessel Master) may be required for operations managers.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by a critical need for younger leaders to replace a retiring generation of professionals:
Alberta: High Demand. Managers here earn some of the highest wages in Canada, with a median of $50.48/hr ($105,517/year), driven by the energy sector and major logistics hubs in Calgary and Edmonton.

Ontario: Very Good prospects. As the industrial and retail heart of Canada, Ontario has the highest concentration of transportation managers, with steady demand in the GTA for freight and municipal transit experts.

New Brunswick & Atlantic Canada: Strong Demand. Driven by port expansions and a 10-year outlook projecting hundreds of openings due to retirements. The median wage in New Brunswick is approximately $45.67/hr.

National Trend: The Transportation Management System (TMS) market in Canada is projected to grow by 16.6% CAGR starting in 2026, increasing the demand for managers who can implement digital tracking and automated scheduling.
Why Work in Canada as a Transportation Manager?
Strategic Impact: Lead the digital transformation of Canada’s logistics, using telematics and AI to enhance safety and efficiency.
Excellent Benefits: Over 96.6% of managers in this NOC receive comprehensive non-wage benefits, including pension plans and full medical/dental coverage.
Cross-Sector Versatility: Opportunities span across airlines, railways, trucking companies, shipping lines, and large-scale retail distribution centers.
Priority for PR: In February 2026, Canada announced new Express Entry categories specifically targeting "Transport Occupations" and "Senior Managers with Canadian work experience."
pathways: Express Entry (2026 Transport Category): As of February 2026, IRCC has introduced a specialized category-based draw for transport occupations. Managers with 6+ months of experience in the sector may receive invitations with lower CRS scores.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A one-time initiative to transition up to 33,000 temporary residents to PR. Transportation managers with established roots in Canada are primary targets.
OINP Human Capital Priorities: Periodically targets managers in the logistics and manufacturing sectors to support Ontario's supply chain.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: A high-success route for those with a job offer in Vancouver’s massive maritime or air transport sectors.
